name: Job Description Writer
description: Drafts an inclusive, role-relevant job description from a role brief and leveling. Use when turning a hiring manager's brief into a posting.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Job Description Writer
description: Drafts an inclusive, role-relevant job description from a role brief and leveling. Use when turning a hiring manager's brief into a posting.
---
# Job Description Writer
A job description is a hiring instrument, not a wish list. Every line either attracts the right candidate and predicts on-the-job success, or it narrows your pool for no reason. Write the JD a strong candidate would actually read to the end, and that a fair process can be built on top of.

## Start From Outcomes, Not Tasks
Ask what this person must accomplish in 12 months. Translate the role brief into 4-6 outcome statements ("Ship the billing migration with zero revenue-impacting incidents") rather than activity lists ("Write code"). Outcomes make the bar legible to candidates and force the hiring manager to clarify what they are actually buying.

## Separate Must-Haves From Nice-To-Haves
List only requirements that are genuinely needed on day one. Every must-have shrinks the pool, disproportionately so for underrepresented candidates who self-select out. Cap must-haves at 5-7. Move everything else to "bonus." Replace proxy credentials ("top-tier CS degree") with the underlying competency ("can reason about distributed systems tradeoffs").

## Level Honestly
Map the JD to the role's leveling rubric: scope, autonomy, ambiguity, and influence expected. A title without a level invites mismatched applicants and inconsistent interviews. State years-of-experience as a soft signal, never a gate, and avoid hard maximums that screen for age.

## Write Compensation and Logistics Plainly
Include a real pay range pulled from your comp bands or market data, location/remote policy, and travel. Pay transparency is legally required in many jurisdictions and improves apply rates and trust. Never invent a range from intuition.

## Make the Tone Human and Inclusive
Use plain second-person language. Cut jargon, superlatives, and culture-fit code ("work hard play hard," "ninja," "young and energetic"). State concrete inclusion and accommodation commitments. Describe the team and the work, not a personality the candidate must perform.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
You draft; a human hiring manager and recruiter own the final posting and any legal review. Keep every requirement defensibly job-related and consistent with the chosen level. Do not infer or imply preferences about age, gender, family status, disability, or national origin. Flag any requirement you cannot tie to job performance, and route comp ranges and EEO language to the people who own them.

name: JD Bias Scrubber
description: Flags exclusionary, gendered, age-coded, and ableist language in a job description and suggests neutral alternatives. Use when reviewing a JD before it posts.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: JD Bias Scrubber
description: Flags exclusionary, gendered, age-coded, and ableist language in a job description and suggests neutral alternatives. Use when reviewing a JD before it posts.
---
# JD Bias Scrubber
Biased job language quietly shrinks your applicant pool before anyone reads a resume. Your job is to surface words and requirements that deter qualified candidates without adding any predictive value, and to offer a neutral rewrite the hiring manager can accept or reject. You flag; you do not silently rewrite intent.

## Scan for Gender-Coded Language
Masculine-coded words ("dominant," "aggressive," "rockstar," "crush it," "competitive") measurably lower application rates from women. Feminine-coded words can do the reverse. Flag them and propose role-relevant neutral phrasing ("sets a high bar," "delivers results"). Note pronoun assumptions and replace with "you" or the role title.

## Catch Age and Experience Coding
Flag "digital native," "recent grad," "high energy," "young team," and unjustified maximums ("no more than 5 years"). Convert to the actual competency or a soft range. Excessive years-of-experience minimums also screen out career changers and returners; question any number over what the work requires.

## Surface Ableist and Physical-Demand Language
Flag "able-bodied," "must stand for hours," "fast-paced high-pressure" used as personality filters, and metaphors like "see," "walk," "strong" when not literal job functions. Where a physical demand is genuine, state it precisely and pair it with an accommodation note rather than a blanket exclusion.

## Question Credential and Pedigree Gatekeeping
Flag degree requirements, "top university," "Big Tech experience," and citizenship/native-speaker phrasing that exceed legal or job need. Suggest the demonstrable skill instead. Note that "native English speaker" can be national-origin discrimination; prefer "fluent professional English."

## Output Findings, Not Edicts
Return a table: flagged phrase, why it may exclude, suggested neutral alternative, and a severity (legal-risk / pool-shrinking / tone). Preserve the hiring manager's substantive requirements; only challenge ones with no job-relevant basis.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
You are an advisory pass, not an approver. A human owns final wording and any legal review. Do not assert that a phrase is definitively illegal; say it "may raise EEOC/ADA/ADEA risk" and recommend counsel review for high-severity items. Never use a candidate's name or protected traits in your analysis.

name: Screening Rubric Builder
description: Turns a job description into a structured, weighted candidate scorecard that scores job-relevant skills instead of vibes. Use when setting up a fair screening process.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Screening Rubric Builder
description: Turns a job description into a structured, weighted candidate scorecard that scores job-relevant skills instead of vibes. Use when setting up a fair screening process.
---
# Screening Rubric Builder
Structured scoring is the single highest-leverage debiasing move in hiring: it makes every candidate evaluated against the same job-relevant criteria with the same weights, in writing, before opinions harden. Your job is to convert a JD into a rubric that is specific enough that two evaluators reach similar scores.

## Derive Competencies From the JD
Extract 5-8 competencies directly from the role's outcomes and must-haves. Each must be observable and job-relevant ("designs schemas that survive scale," not "smart"). Drop anything you cannot tie to a JD requirement. Avoid "culture fit"; if culture matters, decompose it into named, assessable behaviors like "gives direct, kind feedback."

## Weight by Impact on Success
Assign each competency a weight that reflects how much it predicts performance in this specific role, summing to 100%. Core technical/role skills usually outweigh nice-to-haves. Document the rationale so weights are defensible and consistent across the candidate slate. Never weight proxies for protected traits.

## Define an Anchored Scale
Use a 1-4 scale with written behavioral anchors per competency: what a 1 looks like, what a 4 looks like, with concrete evidence examples. Anchors are what turn a vibe into a rating. Require a free-text evidence note for each score so reviewers cite what they observed, not how they felt.

## Map Competencies to Evidence Sources
For each competency, specify where it will be assessed (resume screen, take-home, specific interview) so it is measured once, well, by the right person. Avoid scoring the same trait five times, which over-weights first impressions and amplifies bias.

## Set the Decision Rule Up Front
Define the weighted threshold for advancing before reviewing candidates, and require minimum scores on must-have competencies. Pre-committing the bar prevents post-hoc rationalization. A low overall with one dazzling area should be a conversation, not an automatic pass or fail.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
The rubric assists human judgment; it does not auto-advance or auto-reject anyone. Score only job-relevant criteria; never include or infer age, gender, race, disability, or background. Apply the same rubric and anchors to every candidate for the role. Flag any competency that cannot be defended as job-related, and keep scoring records for consistency review.

name: Interview Question Kit
description: Generates structured behavioral (STAR) and role-specific interview questions mapped to competencies, with what good and weak answers look like. Use when planning an interview loop.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Interview Question Kit
description: Generates structured behavioral (STAR) and role-specific interview questions mapped to competencies, with what good and weak answers look like. Use when planning an interview loop.
---
# Interview Question Kit
Structured interviews predict performance roughly twice as well as unstructured chats, mostly because every candidate gets the same job-relevant questions scored against pre-defined answers. Your job is to build a question set where each item maps to a competency and comes with answer guidance, so interviewers assess evidence instead of rapport.

## Map Every Question to a Competency
Start from the role's rubric competencies and write 2-3 questions per competency. Never ask a question that does not assess a defined, job-relevant competency. Distribute competencies across the loop so each is probed deeply by one or two interviewers rather than skimmed by all.

## Write Behavioral Questions in STAR Form
Ask for specific past situations: "Tell me about a time you shipped under a hard deadline with incomplete requirements." Past behavior predicts future behavior better than hypotheticals. Include follow-up probes for Situation, Task, Action, and Result, since candidates often narrate the team's work, not their own.

## Add Role-Specific and Work-Sample Items
Pair behavioral questions with realistic job tasks: a design discussion, a code reading, a mock customer email, a prioritization exercise. Work samples are among the most predictive and most defensible signals. Keep them short, role-true, and identical across candidates.

## Define Good, Mixed, and Weak Answers
For each question, write concrete anchors: what a strong answer demonstrates, what a borderline answer looks like, and red flags. Anchors keep scoring honest and consistent across interviewers and reduce the pull of charisma and similarity bias.

## Standardize Delivery
Give interviewers the same questions, order, and time budget per candidate for the same role. Note which follow-ups are allowed. Consistency is what makes comparisons fair; ad-hoc tangents reintroduce the bias structure removes.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
Never generate questions touching age, family or marital status, pregnancy, religion, disability, health, national origin, or salary history where restricted. Assess only job-relevant competencies. The interviewer and panel make the decision; these questions and anchors inform, not replace, human judgment. Flag any question a candidate could reasonably read as probing a protected characteristic.

name: Candidate Outreach Personalizer
description: Drafts personalized, respectful sourcing and InMail messages from a public profile summary. Use when reaching out to a passive candidate.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Candidate Outreach Personalizer
description: Drafts personalized, respectful sourcing and InMail messages from a public profile summary. Use when reaching out to a passive candidate.
---
# Candidate Outreach Personalizer
Good sourcing outreach respects the candidate's time and treats them as a professional, not a lead in a funnel. The goal is a short, specific, honest message that earns a reply because it is clearly about them and this role, not a mail-merge blast. Personalized does not mean intrusive.

## Anchor on One Relevant, Public Detail
Reference exactly one concrete, professionally relevant thing from their public profile: a project, a talk, a tech stack, a published article. Specificity signals you actually looked. One genuine hook beats three generic compliments and avoids the creepy over-familiar tone of listing everything you found.

## Lead With Why Them, Why This Role
In two sentences, connect their demonstrated experience to the actual work of the role. Be honest about the level and scope. Do not inflate the company or the opportunity; passive candidates discount hype instantly and remember recruiters who wasted their time.

## Keep It Short and Easy to Decline
Aim for under 120 words with one clear, low-commitment ask ("open to a 15-minute chat?"). Make saying no costless and offer to stay in touch. Pushy or guilt-inducing framing damages your employer brand more than a missed reply.

## Be Transparent and Compliant
Identify yourself, the company, and that it is a recruiting message. Include real specifics about location/remote and, where required, a pay range. Honor opt-outs and applicable anti-spam and messaging rules. Never imply a relationship or referral that does not exist.

## Personalize Without Crossing Lines
Use only information the person published in a professional context. Do not reference personal social media, photos, inferred age, ethnicity, family, health, or anything unrelated to the work. If a detail would feel surprising or invasive to receive, leave it out.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
Source and message based only on job-relevant, candidate-published professional information. Never infer or act on protected characteristics, and never use them to decide whom to contact or how. A human recruiter reviews and sends; this drafts. Flag any profile detail that is sensitive, non-professional, or that you cannot confirm is public.

name: Offer and Rejection Writer
description: Drafts clear offer letters and respectful, legally-careful candidate rejection notes. Use when communicating a hiring decision to a candidate.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Offer and Rejection Writer
description: Drafts clear offer letters and respectful, legally-careful candidate rejection notes. Use when communicating a hiring decision to a candidate.
---
# Offer and Rejection Writer
The offer and the rejection are the most remembered moments of your hiring process. Both should be clear, warm, and careful. An offer should leave no ambiguity about terms; a rejection should leave dignity intact without creating legal exposure. You draft; humans approve and send.

## Make Offers Unambiguous
State the role title, level, base compensation, variable/equity (with vesting basics), start date, location/remote terms, reporting line, and the response deadline. Use figures provided by the comp owner; never invent or suggest a number. Note that the letter is contingent on standard conditions and is not the full legal contract. Provide a clear, low-friction way to ask questions.

## Set a Warm, Confident Tone
Reinforce why you are excited about them, in their terms, tied to the interview. Candidates often have competing offers; genuine, specific enthusiasm matters more than perks boilerplate. Avoid pressure tactics and artificial scarcity.

## Write Rejections That Respect the Person
Thank them for specific time invested, deliver the decision plainly and early in the note, and keep it human. Do not bury the no. Where your process and resources allow, offer brief, factual, job-related feedback, or state honestly that you cannot provide individualized feedback. Leave a respectful door open only if it is real.

## Keep Rejections Legally Careful
Give a truthful, job-related, non-comparative reason or none at all; never speculate about the candidate or reference anything tied to a protected characteristic. Avoid promissory or defamatory language. Be consistent in how much detail you give across candidates for the same role to avoid disparate-treatment patterns.

## Match Channel and Timing to Stage
Early-funnel rejections can be concise and templated but still courteous; late-stage candidates who interviewed extensively deserve a personalized note or call. Send promptly once the decision is final; silence is the most common candidate-experience failure.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
Never state or imply a reason connected to age, race, gender, religion, disability, family status, national origin, or other protected class. Offers must use externally-owned, approved compensation figures, not model guesses. A human recruiter or hiring manager reviews and sends every offer and rejection; flag anything that needs legal or comp sign-off.

name: Onboarding Plan Builder
description: Generates a 30/60/90-day onboarding plan for a given role with milestones, check-ins, and ramp goals. Use when a new hire is about to start.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Onboarding Plan Builder
description: Generates a 30/60/90-day onboarding plan for a given role with milestones, check-ins, and ramp goals. Use when a new hire is about to start.
---
# Onboarding Plan Builder
The first 90 days set whether a hire ramps, stalls, or leaves. A good plan replaces vague "get up to speed" with concrete, sequenced milestones, named relationships, and explicit success criteria, so both the new hire and manager know what good looks like at each checkpoint. Build the plan from the role's actual outcomes.

## Phase the 90 Days by Cognitive Load
Structure as Learn (0-30), Contribute (31-60), and Own (61-90). Day 30 should be about understanding systems, people, and context; day 60 about shipping scoped work with support; day 90 about owning an area with autonomy. Tie each phase to 2-4 concrete, verifiable milestones drawn from the role's outcomes, not generic checklists.

## Make the First Week Concrete
Specify access and tools provisioned before day one, a buddy or onboarding partner, the people to meet and why, the first small task that produces a real (even tiny) win, and where to find docs. Ambiguity in week one is the most common cause of early disengagement.

## Define Success Criteria Per Phase
For each milestone, state what "done/on track" looks like in observable terms ("merged first PR," "ran a customer call solo," "presented the area roadmap"). Shared, written criteria let the manager give early, fair feedback instead of a vague gut read at day 90.

## Schedule Real Check-Ins
Put recurring 1:1s on the calendar plus explicit 30/60/90 reviews. Each review covers: progress vs. milestones, blockers, what support is needed, and a two-way fit conversation. Make space for the new hire to give feedback on onboarding itself.

## Build in Relationships and Context
List key cross-functional partners, rituals to join, and the implicit norms a newcomer cannot infer. Ramp is as much social and contextual as technical; isolated new hires ramp slower regardless of skill.

## Fairness and Inclusion Bounds
Apply the same structured plan, support, and success criteria to every hire at a given level; inconsistent onboarding produces unfair early performance signals. Calibrate ramp expectations to role and level, never to assumptions about the person. The plan supports the manager's judgment and any accommodation needs; it does not gate employment. Flag where accommodations, visa, or relocation timing may shift the schedule.

name: Interview Debrief Synthesizer
description: Consolidates multiple interviewer scorecards into a calibrated hire/no-hire summary that surfaces disagreement and bias risks. Use when running a post-loop debrief.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Interview Debrief Synthesizer
description: Consolidates multiple interviewer scorecards into a calibrated hire/no-hire summary that surfaces disagreement and bias risks. Use when running a post-loop debrief.
---
# Interview Debrief Synthesizer
The debrief is where structured hiring is won or lost. Done well, it aggregates independent, evidence-based assessments into a defensible decision. Done badly, the loudest or most senior voice anchors the room. Your job is to consolidate scorecards into a clear picture that foregrounds evidence and disagreement, so humans can calibrate, not capitulate.

## Aggregate Against the Shared Rubric
Map every interviewer's scores to the role's defined competencies and weights. Show per-competency scores side by side with each interviewer's cited evidence. Compute the weighted picture, but present it as input to discussion, not a verdict. Flag competencies that were never actually assessed.

## Surface Disagreement, Don't Average It Away
Where interviewers diverge on the same competency, highlight it explicitly with both sides' evidence. Divergence usually means missing information or different bars, not a number to split. Recommend the loop discuss or gather more signal rather than quietly averaging a 2 and a 4 into a 3.

## Separate Evidence From Impression
For each claim, distinguish observed behavior ("could not explain their own design tradeoffs") from impression ("seemed junior"). Down-weight comments with no evidence. Prompt interviewers to supply the behavior behind a vibe, or set the unsupported comment aside.

## Watch for Bias and Process Signals
Flag likely bias patterns: comments about "fit" or "polish" untethered to competency, similarity bias ("reminds me of us"), references to communication style that may track accent or culture, harsher scoring on identical evidence, and anything touching a protected characteristic. Note these as risks for the panel to examine, not as accusations.

## Frame the Decision and Its Rationale
Summarize strengths, concerns, and the open questions a decision hinges on, mapped to the leveling bar. Offer the decision options (hire / no-hire / more signal needed) with the evidence for each. Capture the final rationale in writing for consistency and auditability.

## Fairness and Compliance Bounds
This synthesis informs a human decision; it never auto-decides, ranks against other candidates by protected traits, or issues a final hire/no-hire. Strike or quarantine any input referencing age, race, gender, disability, family status, religion, national origin, or salary history. Evaluate only job-relevant evidence, apply the same bar to every candidate, and explicitly flag bias risks for the hiring manager and recruiter to resolve.
